content_en:
Nihayat al-Muhtaj achieved canonical status as one of the two supreme references of the later Shafi'i school, the other being Tuhfat al-Muhtaj by Ibn Hajar al-Haytami. The division of the Shafi'i world into those who follow ar-Ramli's positions and those who follow Ibn Hajar's positions — commonly understood as the Egyptian school versus the Hijazi school — gave the Nihaya a defined constituency and a clear role within the larger tradition. In Egypt, Sudan, and parts of the Levant, ar-Ramli's positions were considered authoritative. In the Hijaz, Yemen, and much of Southeast Asia, Ibn Hajar's Tuhfa was preferred.

This division did not mean that Shafi'i scholars ignored the other commentary. On the contrary, advanced scholars were expected to know both works and to navigate the disagreements between ar-Ramli and Ibn Hajar intelligently. The production of comparison works — texts that systematically listed the points of difference between the two commentaries — became a distinct genre within the Shafi'i tradition, indicating the centrality of both texts to the curriculum.

The Nihaya generated its own secondary literature. Scholars wrote hashiyas on it, the most notable being the hashiya of ash-Shabrikhiti and that of ar-Rashidi. These super-commentaries extended ar-Ramli's analysis and addressed questions he had left open. The existence of this secondary literature confirms the Nihaya's canonical position: scholars engaged with it as a living text, not merely a historical artifact.

Ar-Ramli's influence on legal practice in Egypt was direct. His fatwas shaped the decisions of courts, and his positions on commercial, family, and endowment law influenced how contracts were drafted and disputes resolved. In this sense the Nihaya was not merely an academic text but a practical legal tool, consulted by judges and notaries as well as by scholars and students. Ar-Ramli's long career in Egypt, spanning decades of active teaching and fatwa-issuing, ensured that his positions were not abstract formulations but answers to the real legal questions that Egyptian Muslim society was generating. This grounding in practice gave Nihayat al-Muhtaj a concreteness and utility that purely academic legal works often lack, and it helps explain why the text retained its practical authority alongside its scholarly prestige for so many generations.
